authoritative_sources.title: Department of Cannabis Control Medicinal & Adult Use Cannabis Regulations
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This is the official California regulator-issued rule set defining track-and-trace, testing, quality-assurance review, shipping manifests, remediation, packaging, and manufacturer batch-record obligations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: lane-wide processing control framing, track-and-trace handling, testing, QA review, remediation, and transfer readiness

authoritative_sources.title: Product Quality Plan (PQP)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DCC's PQP guidance is an official cannabis-manufacturing workflow source for product-specific hazard analysis, preventive controls, monitoring, and failure handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: product-specific readiness review and in-process hazard control

authoritative_sources.title: Batch production record checklist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DCC's batch production record checklist is an official lane-specific workflow reference translating cannabis batch-record requirements into concurrent documentation, ingredient, equipment, packaging, and QC evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: concurrent batch execution and batch-history capture

authoritative_sources.title: Cannabis manufacturer: Self-inspection checklist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DCC's self-inspection checklist is an official public workflow and control reference for timely track-and-trace entry, reconciliation, transfer manifests, surveillance, and other inspection-ready operating duties on cannabis manufacturing premises.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: inspection readiness, inventory reconciliation, and transfer preparation

authoritative_sources.title: Closed-loop extraction system checklist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DCC's closed-loop extraction checklist is an official cannabis-specific safety reference for volatile-solvent extraction equipment, certification, and operating constraints that materially limit what processing can advance safely.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: extraction-safety prerequisite review and safety-blocker escalation

authoritative_sources.title: FAQ | Administrative Order No. 4
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official Massachusetts regulator FAQ clarifies that one representative test sample must go to one testing laboratory for a complete compliance panel and one final COA for each production batch.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: testing handoff, compliance panel control, and COA evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Cannabis Control Commission Issues New Order On Testing Methods
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official Massachusetts regulator order summary establishes that labs must upload digital COAs, testing methods, and remediation-step information into the state's seed-to-sale system, making COA evidence operationally central.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: COA retrieval, testing-method transparency, and remediation-evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Bulletin | Metrc Fee Increases | December 23, 2024
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official Massachusetts regulator bulletin explicitly identifies Metrc as the Commonwealth's seed-to-sale tracking system of record for compliance with testing and other public-health and safety protocols.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: systems-of-record identification and seed-to-sale dependency review

workflow_stages.name: Manufacturing intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm jurisdiction, license type, product family, source package lineage, approved protocols, facility readiness, local safety approvals, and governing systems before processing work advances.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Batch execution and concurrent record capture
workflow_stages.description: Use master manufacturing protocols, batch production records, PQP monitoring steps, equipment references, and concurrent operator initials to control in-process manufacturing or remediation work.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Testing, hold, and remediation routing
workflow_stages.description: Move completed or in-question batches into the correct testing, quarantine, failed-panel, or remediation path while preserving single-batch COA integrity and formal approval ownership.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Packaging, labeling, and seed-to-sale reconciliation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm packaging counts, label references, UID assignments, inventory reconciliation, and manifest readiness before product is treated as transfer-ready or complete.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence and safety or authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Require cannabis-specific proof of final records, QA disposition, COA support, seed-to-sale events, and safety posture before completion; escalate legal, release, remediation, or extraction-safety decisions instead of absorbing them.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: work orders, source package UIDs, production batch numbers, and lot identifiers
master manufacturing protocols, product quality plans, and concurrent batch production records
ingredient, component, label, packaging, and yield records
track-and-trace package tags, event logs, transfer manifests, and reconciliation outputs
quality-control monitoring logs, sanitation or calibration records, and deviation or CAPA notes
test sample records, certificates of analysis, quality-assurance review outputs, and hold or quarantine records
extraction equipment certifications, volatile-solvent safety records, surveillance references, and local approval evidence
waste logs, completion signoffs, and escalation packages
systems_of_record: manufacturing execution or batch record repository
laboratory information management system and COA repository
inventory or ERP records for components and packaging
label master data or document-management system

allowed: classify cannabis processing work as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked, on hold, remediation-pending, packaging-ready, transfer-ready, or complete based on documented execution state and evidence
request or validate the minimum tenant facts, batch records, source-package lineage, and systems-of-record evidence needed to continue safely
prepare evidence-backed status updates, exception notes, completion records, and escalation packages without overstating legal or licensed authority
escalate: final quality release, legal interpretation, remediation-plan approval, or policy-override decisions
formulation, potency, label, or packaging-claim changes that require quality, compliance, or product authority
jurisdiction-specific enforcement, recall, or regulator-facing commitments
refuse: start or continue processing when required jurisdiction, license, batch-record, track-and-trace, testing, or safety evidence is missing and would change the outcome
mark cannabis processing work complete or transfer-ready without authoritative COA, QA, packaging, manifest, or seed-to-sale closure records
treat cannabis processing as generic manufacturing when batch tags, COAs, remediation paths, or extraction controls materially change the answer

domain_failure_modes: a batch advances without authoritative source-package or UID lineage because seed-to-sale evidence was not retrieved
processing continues even though batch production records or master manufacturing protocols are incomplete or not recorded concurrently
quality or testing hold requirements are bypassed and product is treated as release-ready without an authoritative COA or QA review
failed testing or remediation pressure is treated as a local schedule problem rather than a formal hold-and-escalate event
volatile-solvent extraction proceeds without the required equipment certification, safety controls, or local approval context
packaging and labeling activity is treated as cosmetic even though serving-size, potency, or label rules could materially change legality or safety
systems of record disagree on package counts, transfer readiness, or completion status and the lane forces closure anyway
the lane drifts into legal, compliance, engineering, or final release authority

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific timing of testing, QA review, and transfer-ready status
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: California uses a distributor-mediated testing and QA review path, while Massachusetts centers testing and COA evidence in its seed-to-sale system. The pack treats jurisdiction and license architecture as mandatory runtime facts before deciding whether a batch can be packaged, transferred, or closed.

Public cannabis regulators differ on what remediation steps are allowed, when department approval is required, and whether a previously tested item must be resubmitted after movement or rework. The pack therefore requires the tenant to provide the active jurisdiction, SOP, and approval map instead of guessing a remediation path.

notes: The governing records for this lane are cannabis-specific: source package identifiers, track-and-trace events, batch production records, product quality plans, COAs, QA review outputs, transfer manifests, and extraction-safety controls rather than generic manufacturing records alone.

The lane stops before legal interpretation, final quality release, remediation approval, formulation or label authorization, and extraction-safety engineering decisions even when throughput pressure is high.
